Sports.title: Galopin Des Champs ruled out of Cheltenham Festival as Willie Mullins gives update
Sports.content:
Galopin Des Champs ruled out of Cheltenham Festival as Willie Mullins gives update

Galopin Des Champs will not run in this year's Cheltenham Festival with Willie Mullins confirming that the 2023 and 2024 Gold Cup winner has suffered a setback.

Last year's runner-up is out for the season with Mullins telling the Sporting Life: "Unfortunately Galopin Des Champs has been ruled out for the rest of the season.

"After working very well on Thursday morning he wasn't right on Friday morning and will miss the Gold Cup and the other spring Festivals."

The Audrey Turley-owned gelding was second to Gavin Cromwell's Inothewayurthinkin a year ago when bidding to join the likes of Arkle and Best Mate as a three-time winner of the Gold Cup.

In two starts so far this season, the ten-year-old had been third on both occasions, in the Savills Chase on his reappearance and most recently to stablemate Fact To File in the Irish Gold Cup.

The Gold Cup betting now has Fact to File and Gaelic Warrior as the joint favourites at 7/2, with Jango Baie (9/2) and Harry Redknapp's The Jukebox Man 5/1.

Inothewayurthinkin is 15/2 to repeat his success of last year. Mullins' Galopin Des Champs bombshell comes not long after jockey Paul Townend backed the three-time Irish Gold Cup wnner to make more history.

FREE William Hill Shop Bets inside your Mirror every day of Cheltenham Don't miss a FREE shop bet inside your favourite racing paper every day of the Cheltenham festival! Plus, get expert inside from our top tipsters with pullouts every day from Tuesday March 10 - Friday March 13, 2026 covering the runners, riders, and everything you need to make the 102nd Cheltenham festival the biggest ever. Find out more here. Bet 18+ only. Terms and conditions apply. See paper for details.

''I know Galopin hasn't been at his brilliant best this season, but I don't think he's finished yet by any stretch of the imagination," Townend, a Betway racing ambassador, said.

"He feels in great form at home, and we're all hoping he'll return to his peak on the biggest stage of all.

"Winning any Gold Cup is a massive achievement, but for him to go there, win a third Gold Cup, and regain his crown would be huge for everybody involved with the horse.

"It's probably the most open Gold Cup I can remember, and there are plenty of horses in there you could make a really good case for, but if Galopin can return to his best, then he'd have a massive chance."

The Cheltenham Festival gets underway next week on Tuesday, March 10 with the famous Gold Cup the fifth race on Friday, March 13.

Sports.title: Lewis Hamilton's blunt exchange with Ferrari engineer speaks volumes about Brit
Sports.content:
Lewis Hamilton's blunt exchange with Ferrari engineer speaks volumes about Brit

Lewis Hamilton endured a nightmare debut campaign with Ferrari, but he has already laid down a marker with his engineer ahead of the 2026 campaign

View 2 Images Lewis Hamilton is looking to avenge a disappointing first year at Ferrari (Image: Getty)

Lewis Hamilton experienced a tense interaction with Ferrari engineer Carlo Santi during the inaugural practice session of the 2026 season. The 41-year-old secured second and fourth positions in two practice sessions in Melbourne and was the only driver to rank in the top four across both sessions.

‌



It was an encouraging beginning for the Brit, who had a disappointing first season with the Scuderia last year. Throughout the 2025 campaign, Hamilton had numerous strained exchanges with his race engineer Riccardo Adami, who was moved into a different position within the red brand at the end of the season.

‌



In Australia, Hamilton was paired with Santi, who was appointed as the seven-time world drivers' champion's interim race engineer. During the session, F1 fans got a potential snapshot of things to come between the two.

‌



What F1 are planning with two Grands Prix if Middle East conflict forces them to cancel READ MORE :

Donald Trump-supporting F1 pundit breaks silence after being let go by Sky Sports READ MORE :

Over the car radio, Hamilton said: "I need more feedback of where that four tenths is," to which Santi responded: "Two tenths in the first sector, power limited."

Unhappy with the feedback, Hamilton retorted: "I understand it's two tenths but where is that power limit," with Santi replying: "Power limit is between 2 and 3. Same Second sector. Same Third sector."

JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! All the best sports news and much more on our dedicated Facebook page

‌



This still wasn't the answer the Ferrari star was seeking and in his quest to get what he wanted, he frustratingly replied: "2 and 3 is not helping. Is it apex speed? Is it exit?" to which Santi responded: "It's power limit."

Still not satisfied, Hamilton, who was becoming noticeably more exasperated, said: "That does not help me, mate. Is it the exit or is it the apex? I don't care about power limit."

View 2 Images Hamilton will not be settling for anything less than maximum detail from his engineers (Image: Getty)

‌



In what marked the first indication they were making progress, the Ferrari engineer responded "It's the apex". A more reassured and content sounding Hamilton then enquired: "Okay. Is apex too high or too low? Give me some feedback.."

The exchange highlighted the level of detail Hamilton will be seeking from his race engineers throughout the campaign. Following his frustration last year, which saw him finish 6th - 86 points behind team-mate Charles Leclerc - and achieve zero podium finishes, Hamilton has made it evident he wants to be competing for a record breaking 8th world title.

When asked what his ambition is, Hamilton said: "The goal is to win. That's what everyone's working towards - every team is. But that's our goal.

‌



Formula One 2026: The Ultimate Season Preview – New Rules, New Rivals, New Champion From radical new rules to a new world champion in McLaren’s Lando Norris, the 2026 Formula 1 season promises more drama, surprises, and spectacle than ever before. Don’t miss our essential special edition: your definitive guide to the new era of F1. Buy now

"To maximise on every opportunity, to be lean, to be hopefully fighting in the top group, hopefully in the first races. We don't really know.

"Mercedes looks particularly quick, and I'm not really sure whether we have seen the full unleashed Red Bull yet, so it's really, really exciting.

Article continues below

"But I do know that, whatever the case, I feel like I've got a great group of people behind me who are head down, focused on bringing performance and really maximising from every weekend."

Sports.title: Darts stars separated by referee after UK Open clash gets heated - 'I don't think he was happy'
Sports.content:
Darts stars separated by referee after UK Open clash gets heated - 'I don't think he was happy'

Charlie Manby and James Hurrell faced each other in the UK Open, but things got heated between the two World Championship quarter-finalists

View 2 Images Charlie Manby and James Hurrell came together in Minehead (Image: X/wadeydarts)

World Championship quarter-finalists Charlie Manby and James Hurrell had to be split-up by a referee after their fiery UK Open darts match.

‌



Manby, who made it to the last-eight at Alexandra Palace before being defeated by eventual finalist Gian van Veen, squared off against Hurrell in the initial stage of the "FA Cup of darts". Hurrell, who reached the same stage at Ally Pally but lost to Ryan Searle, appeared to rile up the darts prodigy, who is also a bricklayer by trade.

‌



As 'Champagne Charlie' stepped up to the oche to throw his three match darts, Hurrell was seen putting his fingers in his ears and pulling faces. When Manby hit double four to secure a 6-4 victory, the 41-year-old continued to grimace while laughing.

‌



Sky Sports Darts star's rule change demand after what Luke Humphries did in the Premier League READ MORE :

It's possible Hurrell was annoyed with the fans at Minehead, many of whom were rooting for Manby to win. Whatever the reason for Hurrell's ear-plugging, Manby was clearly not happy with the World No. 50.

The pair exchanged words as Manby collected his darts from the board. From the footage, it's hard to make out exactly what was said.

JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! All the best sports news and much more on our dedicated Facebook page

‌



However, whatever comments were traded between the two, it triggered a heated reaction from both players. As the pair faced off and became increasingly loud, a referee had to step in to persuade Manby to leave.

The atmosphere intensified with several fans shouting "Come on Charlie," which appeared to aggravate Hurrell even more.

As Hurrell, nicknamed 'the Hillbilly', left the stage to audible boos and whistles, Manby turned back to the crowd and celebrated with a defiant fist pump, drawing even greater cheers from the spectators.

‌



Content cannot be displayed without consent

After the match, Manby opened up about the incident and provided his version of events. He said: "I just don't think he was happy with me giving it the biggun when there's a lot riding on it.

"I am mates with Jammo sat on his table for ProTours and get on really well with him. He were giving it to me back. That was it really. There's no friends on the oche.

‌



"If I want to give it the biggun, I'll give it the biggun. There's no restrictions to that. You've got loads of people out there giving it the biggun as well. My fans, his fans, just as much as each other."

View 2 Images Charlie Manby has been tipped to become one of the next big stars of darts (Image: 2025 Getty Images)

This wasn't the first time Manby has been on the oche as chaos ensued on stage. During his Alexandra Palace debut, Manby beat Scottish darts favourite Cameron Menzies in an exciting match.

Article continues below

As the young Englishman approached to shake hands with Menzies, the Scotsman infamously began pounding his drinks table with his fists, cutting his hand open in the process and leaving both Manby and the entire Palace crowd stunned.

Sports.title: Lando Norris forces Max Verstappen into major decision as Red Bull star talks to FIA
Sports.content:
Lando Norris forces Max Verstappen into major decision as Red Bull star talks to FIA

Max Verstappen has been forced to talk to F1's governing body after reigning champion Norris' decision

View 3 Images Lando Norris has taken over Max Verstappen's number this year (Image: ANDREJ ISAKOVIC, AFP via Getty Images)

Max Verstappen will compete with a new car number this season after Lando Norris claimed the number one. Verstappen, who has raced with it for the previous four years as world champion, now changes to three.

‌



Norris, having secured the F1 title last year, has opted to display one on his McLaren. Norris revealed in December that he would swap from number four to one, although drivers are not obliged to do so.

‌



In earlier eras, drivers retained their numbers unless crowned champion but since 2014, the regulations have been relaxed, permitting drivers to change. Verstappen previously competed with 33 at Toro Rosso and Red Bull from 2015 until his transition to number one following his first title triumph.

‌



The Dutchman's shift to number three was approved by Daniel Ricciardo, his former Red Bull colleague, who had utilised that number. The regulations stipulate a number must be unused for two seasons before becoming available.

Ensure our latest sport headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

It has been fewer than two consecutive seasons since Ricciardo's final race using the number three. Verstappen had to request special permission and then confirm it with the governing body, the FIA, before receiving approval for this campaign.

‌



F1 schedule shake-up suggested as events cancelled as conflict escalates READ MORE :

Oscar Piastri and Zak Brown disagree as Australian Grand Prix point made READ MORE :

In an interview with Viaplay about the alteration, Verstappen said: "It won't be number 33. My favourite number has always been three, apart from number one.

"We can now swap, so it'll be number three. The rules state that a number isn't available until it hasn't been used for two consecutive seasons.

View 3 Images Lando Norris made the call in December 2025 (Image: Joe Portlock, Getty Images)

‌



"Number 33 was always fine, but I just like one three better than two. I always said it represented double luck, but I've already had my luck in Formula One."

Verstappen is only the second driver to race under the number three since personalised numbers were introduced in 2014. Previously, numbers were determined by the Constructors' standings or assigned by organisers.

There won't be any other significant number changes among those drivers returning from last season. However, some competitors making a comeback to the sport, and one newcomer, have selected new numbers.

‌



View 3 Images Max Verstappen raced under one for four years (Image: Getty)

Valteri Bottas will adopt 77, a number with which he has long been associated. Sergio Perez is also reuniting with number 11, which he says is a tribute to former Real Madrid and Inter Milan star Ivan Zamorano, his favourite footballer.

British youngster, Arvid Lindblad, is this year's only rookie and he will race under the number 41. Lindblad explains he chose 41 because it is the closest visual equivalent to his initials, AL. He also becomes the first driver to ever use the number, making it unique to him.

Article continues below

JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! All the best sports news and much more on our dedicated Facebook page

The forthcoming F1 season will kick off on March 6 with the Melbourne Grand Prix in Australia. Event coordinators have assured that the recent travel disruption due to the escalating conflict in the Middle East will not affect the year's opening race.

Sports.title: Diogo Jota's wife shared heartwarming family update as eight months pass since car crash
Sports.content:
Diogo Jota's wife shared heartwarming family update as eight months pass since car crash

Diogo Jota's wife has gone on looking after the couple's three children following the former Liverpool and Wolverhampton Wanderers star's tragic passing in July 2025

View 2 Images Diogo Jota's wife has shared an important update (Image: Carl Recine/Getty Images)

Diogo Jota's wife will experience another bittersweet moment when Liverpool face Wolves in the FA Cup on Friday night. Three days ago, both clubs paid tribute to the late footballer with a round of applause on the 18th minute - the same number Jota wore during his time at the Molineux.

‌



Liverpool lost 2-1 in the Premier League fixture and will hope for a better result in the cup competition on Friday. The two sides may be planning another tribute to Jota, eight months on from his tragic death in July 2025.

‌



Jota and his younger brother Andre Silva died in a car crash in Spain on July 3, 2025. The Reds forward had been travelling with his brother in the Zamora region of Sanabria when the devastating accident occurred.

‌



Jota's wife, Rute Cardoso, has shared an update as she continues to raise the couple's family without her husband. Rute shares three children with Jota - two sons and a daughter - and they have been enjoying a holiday in Thailand.

Emotional Rafael Leao describes pain of Diogo Jota death and impact on Portugal team READ MORE :

In a post on social media, Jota's partner shared several images of their time in Phuket, Thailand. These included a snap of the children in a swimming pool and one of Dinis - the couple's eldest son - as he sported Jota's Liverpool top with number 20 on the back.

Ensure our latest sport headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

‌



The family went on an emotional trip to Hong Kong recently where Rute received a heartwarming gift of locals paying tribute to her late husband. In a clip shared online, the 29-year-old can be seen flicking through the book of condolences filled with messages all about Jota.

One message read: "Thanks for everything Diogo, rest easy mate, in loving memory the lad from Portugal."

Content cannot be displayed without consent

‌



Some of Jota's former teammates' partners shared their love for Rute on her latest social media post. Larissa Peereira, the wife of former Liverpool player Roberto Firmino, wrote: "Lindos dé mais," which translates to "so beautiful" in Portuguese.

Julia Vigas, the partner of ex-Red, Thiago, said: "Lindos dé mais," which translates to "too beautiful" in Spanish. Other heartwarming comments read: "Have a wonderful time, beautiful children" and "I wish you a wonderful day with peace and love."

Another added: "Have a beautiful time with your babies, stay safe and strong, take all the pictures you can. You are a beautiful mama."

‌



View 2 Images Diogo Jota tragically died last summer, leaving behind wife Rute and his three children (Image: Andrew Powell/Liverpool FC via Getty Images)

Rute also posted a tribute to Jota on Valentine's Day by posting a photo of her late husband alongside the song, If I Would Have Known, by Kyle Hume. It is unconfirmed whether Jota's wife will be in attendance at the Molineux on Friday night but recently returned to Anfield when the pair played each other in December 2025.

FOLLOW OUR LIVERPOOL FB PAGE! Latest Reds news and more on our dedicated Facebook page

Article continues below

She uploaded a post on Instagram expressing her gratitude to the club after the couple's two sons, Dinis and Duarte, served as mascots. She said: "From the bottom of my heart, thank you to the club and to all the fans for the love, respect and support shown during this incredibly difficult time.

"Your messages and gestures have meant more than words can express."

Sports.title: Wales banned from wearing traditional colours against Ireland in Six Nations clash
Sports.content:
Wales banned from wearing traditional colours against Ireland in Six Nations clash

Wales won't be allowed to wear their traditional red jerseys for their Six Nations clash with Ireland in Dublin on Friday night

View 2 Images Wales have been prohibited from wearing their traditional red kit against Ireland (Image: Dan Istitene, Getty Images)

Wales will be swapping their iconic red jerseys when they face Ireland as Steve Tandy's side must instead wear an alternative kit for their Six Nations showdown in Dublin.

‌



Following their gut-wrenching narrow loss to Scotland last month, Tandy's men take on Andy Farrell's side in the fourth-round opener at the Aviva Stadium. Yet despite their long-standing tradition of playing in red, they have been compelled to make a change on Friday following a landmark ruling last year.

‌



For many years, the combination of Welsh red and Irish green has proved a source of frustration for fans living with colour blindness. The condition affects approximately one in 12 men and one in 200 women in the UK, according to the NHS.

‌



Red-green colour blindness is the most widespread form of the condition. And campaigners have long called upon either Wales or Ireland to change their strip whenever the two sides meet.

JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! Latest news, analysis and much more on Mirror Football's Facebook page

Those appeals were finally heeded last January when World Rugby's colour blindness guidelines were adopted as official policy across all of the governing body's competitions. Teams are now required to avoid kit clashes that could adversely affect colour blind spectators.

‌



Alongside eliminating clashes between red and green, the guidelines also recommend that "one team should play in a dark kit and the other in a light kit" to prevent any further difficulties.

World Rugby also advises teams whose primary kit is predominantly dark should have a lighter alternative strip. And the reverse also applies, which explains why Wales switched their away shirt from black to white last season.

View 2 Images Wales will wear their white alternate kit against Ireland instead (Image: PA)

‌



The regulations also specify that "primary and alternate kits should be able to play against each other without causing a colour blind kit clash." For this reason, Ireland wore a white kit in Cardiff when the two nations clashed in last year's Six Nations.

For this year's match, however, it is Wales who will wear their white alternative strip. This was unveiled last summer and worn during their autumn international victory over Japan.

Last year's change was praised by campaigners including Kathryn Albany-Ward, founder of Colour Blind Awareness. Albany-Ward told the Daily Mail at the time: "Finally, they are going to make sure that colour-blind people can follow the game.

Article continues below

Get the latest England Rugby merch at Fanatics This article contains affiliate links, we will receive a commission on any sales we generate from it. Learn more Various Prices Fanatics Buy Now on Fanatics Product Description The Six Nations is running from February 5 to March 14 and England Rugby fans can get the latest jerseys, hoodies and more at Fanatics.

"Many thousands of fans will be delighted. I hope it is going to continue and isn't a one-off. This benefits everyone - including the players who often don't feel they are able to speak up. If eight per cent of men are affected, then that means there will be two to three players in every squad who are impacted in some way.

"It's just common sense. With so many people in the population affected it never made sense not take care of your players and your fans. It's just a shame that it has taken them this long to get to this stage."

Sports.title: Man Utd reject stuns pundit with 'best performance' he's seen this season
Sports.content:
Man Utd reject stuns pundit with 'best performance' he's seen this season

Chelsea hammered Aston Villa on Wednesday with Alejandro Garnacho leaving his mark on Stan Collymore, who was hugely impressed with the former Manchester United star

View 2 Images Alejandro Garnacho was a standout for Chelsea during their win over Aston Villa

Stan Collymore claims Alejandro Garnacho produced the 'best performance from a wide player he's seen all season' after the Argentine helped Chelsea tear apart Aston Villa on Wednesday.

‌



Garnacho moved to Stamford Bridge in the summer, calling time on his spell with Manchester United, but has been anything but a regular for the Blues. He was given the chance to impress in midweek and aided his side's top five ambitions.

‌



Joao Pedro scored a hat-trick for Chelsea as they returned home with all three points, but Garnacho, who laid on an assist, enjoyed one of his best outings for his new club - and made a mark on Collymore.

‌



The former Premier League striker will have seen a number of live games this term but it was the 21-year-old who left the biggest impression on him as he waxed lyrical about Chelsea and their potential.

He told talkSPORT: "They opened up Aston Villa at will at times. Villa not playing very well, it has to be said, but they did start and they did take the lead but the way that Chelsea responded and the type of footballers they’ve got, excellent technicians, players who can go past people.

Michael Ballack breaks down in heartbreaking video as Chelsea icon speaks out on son's death READ MORE :

"You had Garnacho down the left-hand side, the best wide man performance I’ve seen in the flesh this season. [Malo] Gusto overlapping Reece James down the right, the movement of Joao Pedro, the calm of the midfield enforcer [Moises] Caicedo. It really was a top-drawer performance.

‌



"If Chelsea can keep everyone together and they can continuously forward momentum and get some crucial consistency, I think they could be an exceptional team moving forward."

View 2 Images The winger could see his playing time increase under Liam Rosenior

Garnacho's display could further his hopes with Liam Rosenior now calling the shots. The new man at the helm admitted his minutes had been limited, but was confident the Argentine would go and deliver.

‌



"Alejandro is a very good player," he said before the win. "He played a lot of games before I came in, and he’s played fewer games maybe due to tactical reasons, but this is a really good game for him to play.

"He can affect the game in his way, with the way he attacks. We think he can be a real outlet for us, and he just has to go and enjoy himself, because that’s when he’s at his best."

Sports.title: NHL trade grades: Brayden Schenn costly, but worth risk for Islanders
Sports.content:
Open Extended Reactions

The 2025-26 NHL trade season continues to pick up steam as the deadline looms at 3 p.m. ET Friday.

The day before the deadline, the Colorado Avalanche built up their forward group by adding Nicolas Roy, sending two conditional draft picks back to the Toronto Maple Leafs in exchange. Later, the Dallas Stars traded for Michael Bunting, sending a third-round draft pick to the Nashville Predators. Then, a late-night barrage, as John Carlson, Conor Garland, Logan Stanley and Luke Schenn were all traded.

Some other big deals that have happened so far this season:

Up until Friday's deadline, ESPN reporters Ryan S. Clark, Kristen Shilton and Greg Wyshynski will be grading each side on all of the big swaps, with the latest deals highest up on this page.

Read on for more, and keep this page bookmarked as the trade volume rises throughout the season.

Jump ahead: McCann to SEA

Faulk to DET

Schenn to NYI

Carlson to ANA

Garland to CBJ

Jets' D to BUF

Roy to COL

Bunting to DAL

Dickinson to EDM

Weegar to UTA

Myers to DAL

Kulak to COL

Panarin to LA

Sherwood to SJ

Andersson to VGK

Hughes to MIN

Jarry to EDM

March 6: Kraken add scoring help in Bobby McMann

Everything was quiet with the Seattle Kraken until they traded for Bobby McMann in a deal with the Toronto Maple Leafs.

The Kraken received McMann with the Leafs getting a 2027 second-round pick and a 2026 fourth-round pick.

Here's what it means for both sides.

Kraken grade: B+

McMann was in demand because he provided potential suitors with a 6-foot-2 center who can use his size while also providing a layer of scoring as someone who is one goal away from a consecutive 20-goal campaign.

That's what hypothetically made McMann a strong fit as a third-line center for the teams that were rumored to be interested in him.

Yet the Kraken trading for McMann creates a bit of intrigue around where he best fits within their lineup.

And could it be as their first-line center?

The 19 goals he scored with the Maple Leafs would be the second most on the Kraken's roster behind Jordan Eberle. Keep in mind, he did that while playing 15:19 per game behind Auston Matthews and John Tavares.

Not that the decision to get McMann wasn't a prudent one, because it was a shrewd bit of business by Kraken general manager Jason Botterill.

It's just that the biggest question facing the Kraken with their newest addition is sorting out where he makes the most sense in their lineup and the trickle-down effect that will have with Matty Beniers, Chandler Stephenson and Shane Wright.

Whatever outcome Kraken coach Lane Lambert arrives at comes with knowing he now has more scoring help for a team that is currently in the final wild-card spot in the Western Conference.

Maple Leafs grade: B

McMann was in the final months of a contract that made him a rental. But he was a rental who only carried a $1.35 million cap hit that hypothetically made him rather affordable.

It's what presents the argument of whether the Leafs could have received more for someone that came at a cheap cost and was a proven 20-goal scorer.

The market for forwards suggests that the Leafs might have received a strong return in this trade, considering there were quite a few teams that only got one draft pick back in exchange for a player.

What they received from the Kraken gives the Leafs a total of five draft picks that they've added by moving on from players this week.

Attaining that capital is expected to help rebuild a farm system that has been impacted by years of trading picks. The Leafs have had three drafts since 2021 in which they've selected fewer than five players. -- Clark

March 6: Red Wings bolster D with Justin Faulk

The Detroit Red Wings left it late when they got defenseman Justin Faulk in a trade with the St. Louis Blues in the final minutes before the deadline Friday.

Faulk moves to the Red Wings with defenseman Justin Holl, prospect forward Dimitri Buchelnikov, a 2026 first-round pick and a 2026 third-round pick going the other way to the Blues.

Here's a breakdown of how both sides performed.

Red Wings grade: B+

Faulk was always expected to carry a significant price tag, with the notion that the premium could increase or prove too rich for some teams as the 3 p.m. ET deadline drew closer.

What the Red Wings offered was a package that struck a balance when it came to still retaining the picks that can supplement their farm system while adding the help they need right now.

They also did this without having to part ways with any of their top prospects such as Trey Augustine, Carter Bear, Michael Brandsegg-Nygard, Sebastian Cossa and Nate Danielson.

Faulk was coveted because he provided anyone who was interested a proven top-four right-handed shot defenseman who can play in every situation. That also came with the bonus that the 33-year-old has one more year left on his contract worth $6.5 million.

It's likely that the Red Wings play Faulk on the second pairing alongside Ben Chiarot. But he also gives them another facilitator on the back end beyond what they already have with Moritz Seider.

Seider leads all Detroit defensemen with 41 points; the next closest is Simon Edvinsson with 20 points. Having Faulk, who has 11 goals and 32 points, allows them to bridge that gap with an experienced facilitator.

There's also the value Faulk provides as someone who can eat quite a few minutes on a penalty kill as he's three minutes away from his 11th season of more than 100 short-handed minutes. That could prove useful for a team that came into Friday ranked 18th on the penalty kill with a 78.5% success rate.

Blues grade: A-

Part of what made Colton Parayko declining the potential trade to the Buffalo Sabres earlier in the week rather difficult for the Blues was what they could have potentially gained.

That would have been both draft capital and shedding a significant salary from their books.

What they received for Faulk also allowed them to get a significant amount of draft capital while getting extra money off their books. Of course, the main difference is that Parayko still had four more years left on his contract after this season while Faulk had one.

Getting Faulk off their books allows the Blues to use that money elsewhere in the offseason. As for how they use that money in free agency?

Let's say that's another conversation for another time.

But for now?

What can be quantified is how much the return for Faulk adds to the haul they received from the New York Islanders in exchange for now former captain Brayden Schenn.

The first-round pick they got from Detroit gives them three in the opening round of this year's draft (their own and first-rounders from the Red Wings and the Islanders). Their own should be near the top of the draft as they entered Friday tied for the third-fewest points in the NHL.

In terms of the players they received. Holl is a veteran who has played nearly 400 NHL games but has spent this season in the AHL. He could be used in a fill-in role while Buchelnikov, who was a second-round pick in 2022, is playing in the KHL, where he has 13 goals and 23 points in 39 games for CSKA Moscow. -- Clark

March 6: Islanders get C Schenn from Blues

The New York Islanders acquired St. Louis Blues center Brayden Schenn before Friday's NHL trade deadline, adding a gritty veteran playmaker for their playoff push.

The Islanders sent veteran forward Jonathan Drouin, a 2026 first-round pick, a third-round pick and 6-foot-7 Swedish goalie prospect Marcus Gidlof to the Blues. The first-round pick was one acquired from the Colorado Avalanche in last season's Brock Nelson trade.

Islanders grade: B-

This was not a buyer's market. But we'll get to that.

New York had to upgrade its offense in order to even hint at being a postseason threat. And the Islanders did actually shoot their shot.

Sitting 22nd overall in scoring with 2.89 goals per game, the Islanders have been hurt by a lack of scoring depth in the past. New York is stingy on defense -- allowing the fifth-fewest goals against -- and that's what makes Schenn an ideal fit for the system.

Schenn is a productive two-way center with bona fide playmaking ability and strong defensive skills. He has 12 goals and 28 points in 61 games this season and can slot in almost anywhere new coach Patrick Roy wants to use him (including on either wing). Schenn is physical, smart with the puck and strong in the faceoff dot (at 54.2% this season). He'll also be an option for the Islanders on special teams.

The intangibles are there for Schenn as well -- he's been captain of the Blues since 2023 and will bring championship pedigree from the Blues' Stanley Cup run in 2019.

It's tidy work by New York here to get Schenn, 34, while he's still a good player and has two years remaining on a reasonable contract (at $6.5 million). New York had the draft capital available thanks to an extra first-round pick this year courtesy of trading Brock Nelson to Colorado last season Given how the Islanders have performed this year, it only made sense to try giving them a boost with a trade for Schenn.

However, it also speaks to how tilted the ice was for sellers this year around the NHL. Blues GM Doug Armstrong demanded a significant return for a player in the back half of his career who, while talented, isn't the most dynamic skater or verifiable game changer. And the Islanders included Jonathan Drouin in the package, who, despite scoring only three goals in 55 games, still clocks in at over 17 minutes a game.

It's all a bit risky considering the Islanders aren't a runaway choice to make noise in the playoffs, but if Schenn can be the complementary piece they're hoping for, then New York's opportunity to succeed in the spring goes up.

Blues grade: B+

There was an expectation that St. Louis would be moving a center. Robert Thomas? Jordan Kyrou? Not so much. But Schenn -- that was the deal Armstrong was willing to do. And how could he not, with a return that exceptional?

Schenn had a 15-team trade list attached to his contract, and New York wasn't on it. Armstrong had to get his blessing before going through with the transaction, and it was an excellent one for the Blues to get done. That Schenn -- at his age and with the role he projects to play for the Islanders -- could fetch what he did for a retooling St. Louis team would have been baffling a few years ago. Now, with contract clauses and the salary cap increasing, trade boards are adjusting too.

Ultimately, St. Louis adds necessary draft capital ahead of what might be a busy offseason and a goalie prospect for the cupboards. The Blues spiraled out of contention this season and are in dire need of a revamp as it is. This deal reflects where St. Louis is at, and shucking Schenn gives them more cap space to wield as well. Drouin is also not a rental -- he is signed through next season with a $4 million cap hit -- and can still be a productive piece of St. Louis' bottom six. -- Shilton

March 6: Ducks trade for Carlson

The Anaheim Ducks acquired defenseman John Carlson from the Washington Capitals for a conditional first-round pick in either the 2026 or 2027 NHL draft and a 2027 third-round selection.

How did both GMs do in this swap?

Ducks grade: B

This is what the trade deadline is all about: Huge swings from playoff-starved teams. Anaheim hasn't made the postseason cut since 2018, but head coach Joel Quenneville currently has them second in the Pacific Division (.582 points percentage). Both Stathletes and Money Puck give them around a 93% chance of making the cut, but this is the kind of trade a team makes to close the deal -- and, potentially, make some noise in the Stanley Cup playoffs.

Carlson, 36, is having one of his best offensive seasons over the past five years with 46 points in 55 games -- 37 of them coming at even strength. He has skated to a plus-11 rating and the Capitals are giving up 2.32 goals per 60 minutes when he's on the ice, one of the better marks on the team among defensemen.

All of ESPN. All in one place. Watch your favorite events in the newly enhanced ESPN App. Learn more about what plan is right for you. Sign Up Now

While the Capitals' power play has been oddly underwhelming this season (16.4%, 28th in the NHL), Carlson has long been one of the NHL's better quarterbacks with the man advantage. His 81 power-play points over the past five seasons rank him 15th among all defensemen.

The Ducks' power play has also been middling at 18.2%, which is 23rd in the NHL. Jackson LaCombe leads Anaheim defensemen with 13 power-play points as their PP1 quarterback. No other defenseman on the team has more than three power-play points. So whether it's supplanting LaCombe or bolstering the second power-play unit, Carlson will help.

One assumes Carlson is a short-term pickup. There's no need to have a 36-year-old defenseman blocking the path for one of Anaheim's blue-chippers on the blue line. Plus, GM Pat Verbeek said the Ducks are "very excited to add a Stanley Cup winner to complement our group and make a big push down the stretch," without adding anything about keeping him around beyond that.

Does Verbeek need to do something before Friday's trade deadline to make room for Carlson, or just hang on to everyone with the assumption that Carlson is one and done?

The cost of acquisition was high but worth it for a player of Carlson's talents, experience and potential impact on the power play. It can be debated whether the Ducks are at a point in their maturation as a contender to trade two good draft picks for a player on a short-term contract.

On one hand, I'd argue they aren't. On the other hand, the Pacific Division is so wide open that Anaheim has as good a shot as any of making the Western Conference finals.

Capitals grade: A

When I spoke with Carlson at Capitals training camp, it was clear he wasn't pleased with the uncertainty surrounding his future. Perhaps he sensed that after a remarkable 17-season run with the same franchise, during which he was a Norris Trophy finalist and a Stanley Cup champion, their objectives were no longer aligned. If next season is the first one of the post-Alex Ovechkin Era -- and increasingly, it's starting to seem that way -- Carlson wasn't going to be in those plans.

Still, it's hard to believe that after 1,143 games, the second most played by a Capital behind Ovechkin (1,554 games), Carlson has been traded.

Credit Washington's front office for its self-awareness and lack of sentimentality. The math isn't currently "mathing" for the Capitals in the East, as they're four points out of Boston's final playoff spot, but the Bruins have two games in hand. If opportunities were there to turn aging veterans into future resources, they had to be explored -- not because Washington is giving up on its season, but because it's just good asset management.

Breaking News from Emily Kaplan Download the ESPN app and enable Emily Kaplan's news alerts to receive push notifications for the latest updates first. Opt in by tapping the alerts bell in the top right corner. For more information, click here.

We witnessed that on display when they traded 35-year-old center Nic Dowd, a vital part of their locker room since 2018, to the Vegas Golden Knights for two draft picks. And now we see it with John Carlson.

The conditions on the first-round pick are as follows: Should Anaheim qualify for the 2026 Stanley Cup playoffs, Anaheim will transfer its 2026 first-round pick to Washington. If Anaheim fails to qualify for the 2026 Stanley Cup playoffs, Anaheim has the option to retain its 2026 first-round pick and transfer its 2027 first-round pick to Washington instead.

The Capitals pulled a third-rounder out of the Ducks, too.

This front office has banked a lot of goodwill with us thanks to a series of smart moves (acquiring Jakob Chychrun, getting good value by acquiring Pierre-Luc Dubois) and big swings that just missed -- where would the Caps be right now had they landed free agent forward Nikolaj Ehlers?

There was little risk in moving Carlson if he wasn't in their long-term plans, and the return is really solid for an assumed short-term player with no salary retention. The only risk was whether this move snuffs out any hope the Capitals had in making the playoffs, if only because the fans now understand their current circumstances in the standings and the looming end to a glorious era in Washington hockey. -- Wyshynski

March 6: Sabres land a pair of Jets

Why get one Central Division defenseman when you can get two of them instead?

The Buffalo Sabres landed defensemen Logan Stanley and Luke Schenn from the Winnipeg Jets in exchange for Jacob Bryson, forward Isak Rosen, a 2027 second-round pick and a conditional 2026 fourth-round pick. The Jets retained 50% of Schenn's contract.

Let's look at what this means for both franchises.

Sabres grade: B+

Knowing that St. Louis Blues defenseman Colton Parayko turned down a trade to join the Sabres meant their front office had to move quickly to find an alternative before the 3 p.m. ET cutoff on Friday.

They struck a deal with the Jets to get a pair of rather sizable defensemen who add experience and depth to a unit that now looks even more formidable.

Stanley is having the strongest season of his career. He has nine goals and 21 points, averaging 17:12 in ice time per game. The 6-foot-7, 231-pound Stanley is also adept on the penalty kill.

Schenn will be the Sabres' sixth or seventh option on their blue line. But he's also a two-time Stanley Cup winner, whose postseason experience could prove useful for a franchise that hasn't made the playoffs in 14 years.

After the trade, the Sabres have $5.075 million in deadline cap space, per PuckPedia -- so getting the Jets to retain 50% of the Schenn contract is vital.

Did Buffalo pay too much for a pair of players for the short term? It won't really matter if this duo plays a role in helping the Sabres, who entered Friday in second place in the Atlantic Division, make their long-awaited return to the playoffs -- and perhaps advance beyond the first round.

Jets grade: B+

With the playoffs now seeming out of range, the Jets elected to move on from players in exchange for some draft capital -- and they might not be done yet.

If that trend continues, Gustav Nyquist (who has been dealt on deadline day previously) is one of the options.

As for the players acquired, Bryson gives them a defenseman who can go into their NHL lineup. He's in the final months of a one-year deal worth $900,000.

Rosen is in a different situation. The 22-year-old forward was the 14th overall selection in 2021. He has largely been a prolific AHL player, with just one point in his first 15 NHL games over two seasons to having seven points in 16 games for the Sabres this season.

It's possible that he could continue playing in the AHL for the remainder of this season, unless the Jets wanted to see what he could do at the NHL level. Rosen is also in the final months of his deal and will be a restricted free agent this summer.

The irony in the Jets getting a second-round pick in 2027 in a trade involving Schenn is the fact they dealt their 2026 second-round pick to get him last season from the Pittsburgh Penguins.

The fourth-round pick gives the Jets seven picks for 2026, while the second-round selection gives them six picks for 2027 (with potentially more on the way on Friday). -- Clark

March 6: Blue Jackets add Garland from the Canucks

Technically speaking, the Columbus Blue Jackets did move early on deadline day to get Conor Garland from the Vancouver Canucks.

Garland was moved in the wee hours of Friday morning to the Blue Jackets in exchange for a 2028 second-round pick and a 2026 third-round pick.

Here's a glance at how both teams performed, and what Garland can do for Columbus.

Blue Jackets grade: A-

The impact of this trade could be influenced in the short term by Mason Marchment's status.

Marchment left Thursday's game with a lower-body injury, and Blue Jackets coach Rick Bowness told reporters that the team will know more Friday about the extent of the injury.

Not that the potential absence was the main reason to make a trade, but adding Garland doesn't hurt.

The enhanced ESPN App Watch your favorite events in the newly upgraded ESPN App. Learn more about what plan is right for you. Sign Up Now

Garland hasn't had the easiest season with the Canucks. He has seven goals and 26 points in 50 games -- a pace that would be a career-low nine goals and 35 points. But that's not a unique outcome for a Canucks player, given how poorly the team has performed in general this season.

The version of Garland that Columbus is banking on is the one who has scored more than 19 goals over four of the past five seasons.

It's important to note that Garland is in the final months of his current contract, about to start a new six-year deal worth $6 million annually. He'll be the seventh player on the Blue Jackets' roster to have a contract with three years or more remaining.

Bottom line, there is hope that he can help the Blue Jackets reach the postseason for the first time since the 2019-20 season. Currently, they are a point behind the Boston Bruins for the final Eastern Conference wild-card spot.

Canucks grade: B

Was this enough of a return for Garland?

There are a few reasons to ask this question, especially when comparing this trade to other deals teams have made for forwards.

Vancouver got two draft picks, and that falls in line with what the Nashville Predators (Cole Smith), the Los Angeles Kings (Warren Foegele) and the Toronto Maple Leafs (Nicolas Roy) received in their respective deals.

Yet what makes this different than those other deals are the term Garland has on his future contract. An argument could be made that the Canucks should have pushed for more in return because Garland will be under contract for six more years, giving Columbus cost certainty.

On the other hand, the Blue Jackets helped the Canucks by taking a substantial future salary off their hands, without forcing them to use their final salary retention slot.

Moving on from Garland allows the Canucks to use that retained salary spot to trade another player on their roster who has a sizable contract, such as Jake DeBrusk or Evander Kane.

But adding the two draft picks means the Canucks now have five total picks in the first three rounds of the 2026 draft, and three total picks in each of the first two rounds in 2027 and 2028. -- Clark

March 5: Stars add to forward depth with Bunting

A day after adding defenseman Tyler Myers, the Dallas Stars stayed busy on Thursday, trading for forward Michael Bunting from the Nashville Predators. The Stars sent a 2026 third-round pick the other way to the Predators.

Here's a quick look at what it means, and what kind of value both GMs added to their respective teams.

Stars grade: B+

Bunting gives the Stars another top-nine scoring option, going into a playoff run the length of which might well be determined by scoring depth.

The Stars already have eight players who have reached double figures in goals this season; Bunting gives them a ninth, with 13 goals and 31 points through 61 games.

The veteran could be used in their middle-six winger group, an addition that comes as the Stars are the hottest team in the NHL with a 10-game winning streak.

It's a streak that has been done in parts without three forwards -- Radek Faksa, Roope Hintz and Mikko Rantanen -- on injured reserve, while Tyler Seguin, who last played Dec. 2, will sit out the rest of the season because of a knee injury.

Getting a healthy version of Faksa, Hintz, and Rantanen back into their lineup, in addition to Bunting, adds to the belief that the Stars could reach a fourth straight Western Conference finals and their first Stanley Cup Final since 2020.

Predators grade: B

Losing three straight games and five of their last six was clearly enough for the Predators' front office staff to start thinking about the future.

The Bunting trade is the latest deal in a week that has seen them move on from defenseman Nick Blankenburg, as well as forwards Michael McCarron and Cole Smith.

All of the deals thus far have been made for draft picks, a tidy piece of business for GM Barry Trotz.

The Preds now have 12 picks total for the 2026 draft, including five in the first three rounds. And looking ahead a bit, they now have 33 total selections from the 2026 through 2028 drafts.

Whether they'll end up using all of those picks -- or using them to add players, or as part of a bigger trade involving other current Preds -- remains to be seen. -- Clark

March 5: Avs land Roy from the Maple Leafs

Let's get this out of the way, shall we? The Colorado Avalanche just traded for someone whose last name is Roy.

Now for the actual news: The Toronto Maple Leafs traded Nicolas Roy to the Avalanche on Thursday in exchange for a conditional 2027 first-round pick and a 2026 fifth-round pick.

Here's a quick glance at what it all means for both clubs.

Avalanche grade: A

Adding a third-line center was a priority, and the Avs traded for perhaps the strongest available option ahead of the deadline.

During Roy's time with the Vegas Golden Knights, he emerged into a legitimate bottom-six anchor who could provide a team with more than 10 goals and 30 points per season and could be trusted to play in various game situations.

At 6-foot-4, 201 pounds, he's a player with a physical component to his game, and he can also be used on the penalty kill.

All of ESPN. All in one place. Watch your favorite events in the newly enhanced ESPN App. Learn more about what plan is right for you. Sign Up Now

But what made him particularly attractive to the Avalanche (and others) was what he did during the Golden Knights' Stanley Cup run in 2023, when he had three goals and 11 points in 22 games while playing a versatile role for the champs.

Adding Roy to Nathan MacKinnon and Brock Nelson at the center position adds to the belief that this could be the Avs' year to win the fourth Stanley Cup in franchise history.

What's more, he's in the first of a two-year contract that's worth $3 million annually, so having him will be an asset for Colorado next season as well.

PuckPedia projects the Avs now have $6.94 million in deadline cap space if they want to make one more move.

Maple Leafs grade: A-

Roy landed in Toronto after the Mitch Marner trade and signed a two-year contract. The fact that he was traded away less than a year later says everything about the Leafs' current situation.

Of course, there's something to be said for how they parlayed a player who cost them $3 million for two years into a conditional first-round pick and a 2026 fifth-rounder.

There's no denying that the Leafs are going to be concentrating on their future. Figuring out what path they will take to reach their desired destination is the mystery.

Toronto came into the day without its first-round pick for 2026 or 2027. Both were traded last year; one for Brandon Carlo, the other for Scott Laughton. All those years of trying to push for a deep playoff run meant the Leafs have held on to their first-round pick just twice since 2021. Now they'll at least have one in next year's draft (although it is top-10 protected). The way the Avalanche are built for next season suggests it would take something drastic for the Leafs to not get that pick.

This also makes the fifth-round pick significant. As of now, the Leafs won't pick until the third round and won't pick again until the fifth. Getting an extra fifth-rounder means they'll have four picks at this draft, with two of them originally belonging to other teams.

That's a bit of a difficult spot to be in for a team that went from fighting to win the Eastern Conference to now challenging for a lottery pick, as Toronto entered Thursday with the 12th fewest points in the NHL.

The decision for Leafs GM Brad Treliving to move on from Roy could be just the start. Treliving has a roster that has six players who are entering the final year of their contracts. -- Clark

Why have only one deal between the Chicago Blackhawks and Edmonton Oilers when you could have two in the days before the trade deadline?

The Oilers received center Jason Dickinson and forward Colton Dach in a swap that sent recently waived forward Andrew Mangiapane and a conditional 2027 first-round pick to the Blackhawks. The Blackhawks will retain 50% of Dickinson's salary.

Let's look at what it means for both teams and how each side performed.

Oilers grade: A-

From signing a two-year deal worth $3.6 million annually to being waived months later and being traded days after being waived. This chaotic description of Andrew Mangiapane's 2025-26 campaign further reinforces why Oilers general manager Stan Bowman needed to find answers before the trade deadline.

Not that Mangiapane was expected to solve for all of the Oilers' bottom-six concerns. But what he cost the Oilers -- a team that was already having to carefully manage its salary cap -- meant they were paying for consistency. The version they signed was on pace to score 11 goals -- which would have been the lowest return since his rookie campaign.

Breaking News from Emily Kaplan Download the ESPN app and enable Emily Kaplan's news alerts to receive push notifications for the latest updates first. Opt in by tapping the alerts bell in the top right corner. For more information, click here.

That's what made the need to find an alternative on a cheaper contract rather important. It appears that the Oilers have done that with Dickinson and Dach.

The Blackhawks retaining 50% of Dickinson's salary means the Oilers have two forwards on their roster earning a combined $2.95 million from the team.

It's a savings of more than $500,000 in cap space vs. Mangiapane, with the bonus of Dickinson being an unrestricted free agent at the end of the season while Dach will be a restricted free agent under team control.

Dickinson gives the Oilers someone they can use as their third-line center behind Connor McDavid and Leon Draisaitl. He also provides the Oilers with one more forward who can be used down the middle or on the wing. It's a group that also includes Trent Frederic, Adam Henrique and Ryan Nugent-Hopkins.

The 30-year-old can also kill penalties, as he has logged more than 100 shorthanded minutes for a fourth straight season. Dach, a second-rounder in 2021, has nine points through 53 games in a limited role.

PuckPedia projects the Oilers have $475,000 in cap space after moving Curtis Lazar to LTIR. So the savings accrued from this trade are even more important, with the notion they'll need to clear even more should they seek to make another move by Friday's deadline.

Blackhawks grade: A-

Trading Connor Murphy to the Oilers on Tuesday left the Blackhawks with one salary retention spot remaining, with the other committed to Seth Jones for the next four years after this season.

Any deal that would involve using that final spot needed to be worth it, and what they received for retaining Dickinson's salary was a first-round pick.

Landing the Oilers' first-round pick in 2027 (top-12 protected) has set the Blackhawks up for a sixth straight draft cycle with multiple first-round picks.

The enhanced ESPN App Watch your favorite events in the newly upgraded ESPN App. Learn more about what plan is right for you. Sign Up Now

No, seriously.

They had three first-rounders in 2022 that were used to select Kevin Korchinski, Frank Nazar and Sam Rinzel. They had two first-round picks in 2023 they used to take Connor Bedard at No. 1 and Oliver Moore at No. 19.

Chicago had three more picks in 2024 that it used to take Artyom Levshunov at No. 2 along with Sacha Boisvert (No. 18) and Marek Vanacker (No. 27). The Blackhawks had three more first-round picks in 2025, allowing them to take Anton Frondell at No. 3, Vaclav Nestrasil No. 25 and Mason West at No. 29.

They have two first-round picks in the 2026 draft, with their latest trade setting them up to now have two more first-rounders in 2027.

For those who didn't count all of that, it comes out to a total of 15 first-round picks from 2022 through 2027.

As for Mangiapane? It's possible they could use him on their roster this season and help revive his game. If it works, he can help as they build back into a contender -- or he can be the latest veteran the Blackhawks trade to get more draft capital in return for a trade next season. -- Clark

March 4: Mammoth add Weegar

You might have noticed that there seems to be a theme with defensemen moving ahead of the trade deadline.

MacKenzie Weegar became the latest to join that club with the Calgary Flames trading him to the Utah Mammoth in exchange for defenseman Olli Maatta, prospect forward Jonathan Castagna and three second-round picks in 2026.

Let's examine how both sides did.

Mammoth grade: A-

Mammoth general manager Bill Armstrong was able to achieve two things with this trade: adding a versatile puck-moving defenseman to strengthen his team's top-four setup, and doing it without giving up any of Utah's top prospects.

What made Weegar so important for the Mammoth is that his style fits the profile of what has allowed them to be in a wild-card spot: control.

The Mammoth entered Wednesday ranked fourth in shot-share as one of the best teams in the NHL when it comes to puck possession.

All of ESPN. All in one place. Watch your favorite events in the newly enhanced ESPN App. Learn more about what plan is right for you. Sign Up Now

Couple that with other defensive metrics: They are giving up the fourth-fewest shots per 60, the seventh-fewest scoring chances per 60 and are just outside the top 10 in fewest high-danger chances allowed (12th).

One of the methods in which the Mammoth have become adept at controlling play goes back to having defensemen such as Sean Durzi, John Marino and Mikhail Sergachev -- all of whom have a puck-moving component to their games.

Now they have another in Weegar. The Mammoth entered Wednesday with a shot-share of 53.31% in 5-on-5 play while Weegar's shot-share was 51.44%.

Another item he provides them is an experienced penalty killer. Weegar has shown he can be one of the anchors of a short-handed unit, logging more than 100 minutes in those scenarios in six straight seasons.

Weegar won't address all the issues with the Mammoth's penalty kill, but he was one of three defenseman who logged more than 100 short-handed minutes for a Flames' kill that is seventh in the NHL with an 82.4% success rate. The Mammoth are 22nd at 77.7%.

That's what he provides in the interim. As for the long term? The 32-year-old is in the third year of an eight-year deal worth $6.25 million annually. He becomes the seventh player on the Mammoth to have a contract that's more than three years in length.

And with control being at the heart of why the Mammoth went after Weegar, they could still have even more with PuckPedia projecting they will have $25.7 million in deadline cap space.

Flames grade: B+

The stark reality for the franchise's current condition is that Weegar is the second, top-pairing defenseman the Flames have traded this season (see Rasmus Andersson) with the idea they could trade another top-four option in Zach Whitecloud (who was also part of the Andersson trade).

That's one way of looking at this trade. Another is by comparing what the Flames received compared with other teams that parted ways with one of their top-four defensemen.

Connor Murphy netted the Chicago Blackhawks a 2028 second-round pick. Tyler Myers brought the Canucks a 2027 second-round pick and a 2029 fourth-round pick.

Weegar led to the Flames receiving a player they can use right now in Maatta, a prospect for the future in Castagna and three-second round picks that can be used this summer to strengthen their prospect depth.

The Flames appear to be on top of the leaderboard when it comes to what might be the strongest return a team has received ahead of the 3 p.m. ET Friday trade deadline.

Getting Maatta, who has two more years left on his contract, means the Flames also saved an additional $2.75 million in cap space now that they shed Weegar's contract. They also made a deal work without having to use their third and final salary retention spot -- the others were used in the trades for Andersson and Jacob Markstrom.

Calgary now has six picks in the first two rounds of this year's draft, and eight picks in the first three rounds. That comes with the possibility they could add even more depending upon what happens with the trio of Stanley Cup-winning trade targets on their roster in Blake Coleman, Nazem Kadri and Whitecloud, among others. -- Clark

March 4: Stars add Myers to bolster blue line

It has become an annual tradition: the Dallas Stars adding a big, right-shot defenseman ahead of the trade deadline.

The Stars traded for Tyler Myers on Wednesday, sending the Vancouver Canucks a 2027 second-round pick and a 2029 fourth-round pick. The Canucks will also retain 50% of Myers' salary.

Here's a look at how both front offices performed in the swap.

Stars grade: B

Salary retention is critical for any contending team around the trade deadline. What makes this even more crucial for the Stars is Myers still has a year remaining after this season on a contract worth $3 million annually.

Adding a 6-foot-8, 229-pound, right-shot defenseman that can log more than 20 minutes per game for a $1.5 million average annual value is the sort of value that could help the club in the coming days should general manager Jim Nill elect to make more moves.

PuckPedia now projects that the Stars will have $5.51 million in deadline salary cap space before the 3 p.m. ET Friday cutoff.

Breaking News from Emily Kaplan Download the ESPN app and enable Emily Kaplan's news alerts to receive push notifications for the latest updates first. Opt in by tapping the alerts bell in the top right corner. For more information, click here.

So will this be the move, or rather the first move ahead of the deadline that can help the Stars reach the Stanley Cup Final?

The Stars went down this path back in 2024 when they traded for Chris Tanev. It happened again in 2025 when Cody Ceci was part of the Mikael Granlund trade. Neither addition got them back to the Cup Final.

Myers provides depth and size to a team that already had plenty. The Stars entered Wednesday as the third-tallest and fourth-heaviest roster in the NHL, per Elite Prospects. And that was before the trade.

He has also averaged more than 150 short-handed minutes in six straight seasons, which gives Dallas another option on its 12th-ranked penalty kill (80.6%).

Ultimately, Nill's mission is to win the Stanley Cup, and this trade will influence those chances both this year and potentially next.

play 1:26 Tyler Myers lights the lamp Tyler Myers lights the lamp

Canucks grade: B

Some might debate whether the Canucks could have received more for Myers -- especially when they are retaining 50% of his salary.

But what will dictate how this trade is viewed depends upon what happens between now and when the deadline strikes at 3 p.m. ET Friday.

Myers was one of a handful of right-shot defensemen -- such as Justin Faulk, Dougie Hamilton and Zach Whitecloud -- who are expected to be traded this week.

One challenge facing the Canucks here was attempting to receive substantial draft capital for a player of a certain profile, while also trying to predict the market when there are other options. Vancouver got one data point when Connor Murphy was dealt to the Oilers for a 2028 second-round pick earlier this week.

Another is the fact that Myers had a no-movement clause, meaning there was a limited number of trade partners.

With Vancouver making its trade first, this could set the parameters for the going rate. If and when we see those deals, we'll have a better sense whether the Canucks could have received more.

Bottom line: The goal for the Canucks with this or any trade before the deadline is about positioning toward their future.

Vancouver has two first-rounders and two second-rounders in the 2026 draft. It has six picks in the first four rounds in 2027, and five picks in the first four rounds of 2029.

Moving on from Myers was the first step in what is expected to be an active deadline, with players such as Teddy Blueger, Evander Kane, and David Kampf, among others, set to be unrestricted free agents at the end of the season.

Vancouver now has one final salary retention spot remaining with the idea it could be used to broker a deal for Kane, who is earning $5.13 million this season. -- Clark

Brett Kulak is on the move again this season, with the Colorado Avalanche being his latest employer as part of a trade that saw Samuel Girard and a 2028 second-round pick go to the Pittsburgh Penguins.

Here's a glance at how both teams fared in the deal that saw them each get a new defenseman.

Avalanche grade: B+

As the Avalanche move on from one of the longest-serving players on their roster, it's another example of how multiple things can be simultaneously true.

Girard had one more year remaining on a seven-year contract worth $5 million annually. The Avs signed him to that deal at a time in which he was playing top-pairing minutes. His role, however, has gradually changed over time, with a defense that would see Cale Makar and Devon Toews become arguably the top pairing in the NHL.

Eventually, they added other defensemen such as Josh Manson and Brent Burns, the latter of whom arrived as a free agent last summer, which made the Avalanche stronger on the back end. But it also meant Girard alternated between being a second- or third-pairing option.

All told, it made Girard the Avs' third highest-paid defenseman while playing the fifth-most minutes -- and having the fewest points of any defenseman in the Avs' top-six group.

Getting Kulak allowed them to add a third-pairing defenseman for a fraction of the cost. The veteran is in the final year of a contract that pays $2.75 million annually.

Kulak, who is 6-foot-2, also allowed the Avs to get bigger on the back end now that they have parted ways with Girard, who is 5-10. That now leaves Sam Malinski as the lone defenseman in the Avs' core defensive group that is shorter than six feet. Furthermore, Kulak gives the Avs another option on their penalty kill.

Altogether, it's the type of trade that offers both value and savings that the Avs could possibly apply elsewhere as the NHL trade deadline approaches on March 6.

play 0:41 Brett Kulak scores goal vs. Kraken Brett Kulak lights the lamp for Penguins

Penguins grade: B

The Penguins can use Girard either on their second or third pairing. What makes his situation in Pittsburgh different than that of the one in Colorado comes back to necessity.

Pittsburgh has gone through a revolving door in trying to solidify what first-year coach Dan Muse believes to be his strongest six options on defense on a nightly basis.

Breaking News from Emily Kaplan Download the ESPN app and enable Emily Kaplan's news alerts to receive push notifications for the latest updates first. Opt in by tapping the alerts bell in the top right corner. For more information, click here.

Kris Letang, Erik Karlsson, Ryan Shea and Parker Wotherspoon have been constants. They've each played in more than 50 games this season, and have been instrumental in why the Penguins exit the Olympic break in a playoff spot.

But this has also been a season in which the Penguins have shuffled through nine defensemen to fill those last two spots. Some of the challenges they've faced in that venture has been due to injuries, such as the one that limited Connor Clifton to 26 games this season.

Granted, they might have had their six -- with Kulak -- before the trade. But Girard gives them another proven puck mover from the back end, who further complements what they already have with Letang and Karlsson. Shea, who came into the season with six career points in 70 games, already has 22 in 56 games, while Wotherspoon has set a new personal best with 19 points after having 16 points in 108 career games entering this season.

Then there's the draft capital. Getting that second-round pick from the Avs has the Penguins set up quite well for the next few draft cycles. The Penguins now hold 16 combined picks in the first three rounds between the 2026, 2027 and 2028 drafts, which can be used or moved to acquire more immediate help. -- Clark

Los Angeles Kings acquire winger Artemi Panarin from the New York Rangers for a conditional third-round pick (2026), a conditional fourth-round pick (2028) and forward Liam Greentree. The Rangers retained 50% of Panarin's $11,642,857 cap hit.

Rangers grade: B

I know Rangers fans are going to look at this grade and groan and start chanting "fire Drury" at their screens, but let's consider the facts:

• Panarin had a full no-movement clause. And not one of those NMCs that teams give away like samples at a mall food court, but a legitimate one they needed to give Panarin in 2019 to lure him as a coveted free agent going to a then-rebuilding team.

• He had a desire to go to a team willing to give him a contract extension now, so he wouldn't have to go through the headaches of unrestricted free agency this summer.

• According to NHL sources, he would waive the no-movement clause and sign an extension for only one team: the Kings. (OK, probably two, considering the team Panarin really wants to play for, the Florida Panthers, didn't have anywhere near the cap space flexibility to welcome him to the South Beach party. As one NHL executive speculated, the Panthers not getting deep into the Panarin pursuit probably means they expect Aleksander Barkov to be using his cap space again this season.)

The way the Panarin trade derby worked: Rangers general manager Chris Drury opened the process to teams that were willing to trade for him and extend him, which is what Panarin wanted. They made their pitches, but one source told ESPN that it was essentially a group of teams trying to persuade him not to sign with Los Angeles, the desired location for Panarin and his family. No one was able to get Hollywood off his mind.

So the market was essentially one team. Drury's options were trade Panarin (and retain 50% on his salary, which is fine in a lost season) for whatever the Rangers could conjure up from a team that had all the leverage, or have him walk away for nothing this summer, having already declared he wasn't going to be part of the team moving forward.

All of ESPN. All in one place. Watch your favorite events in the newly enhanced ESPN App. Learn more about what plan is right for you. Sign Up Now

I know this isn't going to be popular, but considering those circumstances, I think the Rangers did OK here.

Greentree was ranked second among the organization's prospects in Corey Pronman's prospect pipeline rankings for The Athletic last August. Greentree was drafted 26th in 2024 and is in his fourth season with the OHL Windsor Spitfires, coming off a 49-goal, 119-point campaign last season.

His calling card is his size: The 20-year-old winger is 6-foot-4 and upwards of 215 pounds. Greentree is a power forward in dimension but not always on the ice, where he is more a skilled winger than a tractor trailer. I've heard comparisons to Matt Boldy stylistically, which the Rangers would take any day.

Greentree has a high ceiling, but there are red flags. His 45 points in 34 games shows regression this season in Windsor. He hasn't played pro hockey yet. Plus, the Rangers haven't exactly had the greatest success in maximizing the potential of their prospects. But they certainly view him as a high-end prospect internally.

The conditional third-rounder will be the better of the Kings' and the Dallas Stars' in the 2026 NHL draft unless Los Angeles wins a playoff round, in which case it becomes a second-rounder. Expect a prayer circle later this season at Madison Square Garden that the Kings don't meet the Edmonton Oilers again in the first round.

The Rangers get a 2028 fourth-round pick if the Kings win two playoff rounds this season. They probably could have asked for more in that scenario, but again, that's the reality of the trade market.

The Washington Capitals, Carolina Hurricanes and Tampa Bay Lightning were among the teams in the mix for Panarin, but he wanted to live and play in Los Angeles, so Drury had no other options.

There are reasons for Rangers fans to chant "fire Drury" to their heart's desire, what with the team last in the East and on its second rebuild in eight years. Bellow until you're hoarse. But save your breath on this deal involving a no-movement clause he didn't hand out and a player wielding it to go to one destination only. There was no winning this trade.

play 0:44 Artemi Panarin capitalizes on the power play Artemi Panarin scores on the power play for New York Rangers

Kings grade: A-

Of course, Panarin's decision to narrow his preferred destinations to Los Angeles and only Los Angeles meant he couldn't exactly dictate terms to the Kings. One NHL executive believes that's why the process went down the wire ahead of Wednesday's Olympic trade deadline: Panarin was weighing longer-term contract options from other teams.

I think the two-year term was Panarin's way of enticing the Kings to trade for him. Sure, they loved his game and wanted to add his star power. But there's a give-and-take here. Any hesitancy about the Kings acquiring a player of his age for this season and four more -- his rumored ask -- was soothed by Panarin opting for a two-year deal.

This trade was already a win for getting a dynamic point-producing winger for conditional picks and an expendable (in their eyes) prospect. To not have to extend a player who turns 35 in October for the term he was reportedly seeking was a huge reason I'm grading this a little higher than I might have otherwise.

Panarin remains an elite playmaking winger. He has 57 points in 52 games on a terrible team this season, after 89 points in 80 games last season. He is sixth in the NHL in points over the past three seasons (266 in 214 games) and seventh in goals (105) during that span -- the kind of elite offensive talent who is as deadly setting up goals as he is finishing chances.

The Kings are 28th in goals per game this season (2.57). Whether that's on coach Jim Hiller's system or a lack of overall talent, bringing Panarin on should obviously trend that higher. If nothing else, a player who is ninth in power-play points over the past three seasons (87) should be a boon to a power play that's clicking at 15.6% this season in L.A.

Holland was hired by the Kings to span two eras: the final season for Anze Kopitar and then the post-Kopitar seasons with a core of Quinton Byfield, Kevin Fiala, Adrian Kempe, Brandt Clarke, however long Drew Doughty wants to play and Darcy Kuemper in goal.

In the short term, Panarin at a minimum gives the Kings a boost to make the playoffs in a competitive race and then a puncher's chance to get out of the first round for the first time since winning the Stanley Cup in 2014. As modest as that sounds, that was essentially the mandate given to Holland.

"When I reflect back on the growth of the Detroit Red Wings when I was there and then I reflect back on the building of the Edmonton Oilers and where they are today, in the Final Four two straight years in a row, you've just got to stick with it," he said at his introductory news conference. "You've got to find a way to win a series, and every time you can get a little more confidence from accomplishing something it builds you going forward. I think you've just got to stick with it. There are no magic wands or tricks."

After this season, they have Panarin at $11 million average annual value with the cap going up and Kopitar ($7 million) coming off it. That's a good chunk of change and does impact their flexibility to do other things, which is concerning because this team needs more than Panarin to challenge for a Stanley Cup. Between Panarin, Fiala and Kempe, the Kings have nearly $30 million on the wing next season and need to replace their No. 1 center. Woof.

But the two-year term, after Panarin was seeking double that? That's a win for Holland and team president Luc Robitaille, who I understand pushed hard for Panarin.

I realize there's a nihilistic response to this trade that asks, "Why make it?" when the Kings aren't nearly as good as the Avalanche, Wild or Stars, and probably not the Golden Knights, and they've lost to the Oilers in four straight seasons. So, what, don't add the sixth-best scorer of the past three seasons for picks you won't miss and an unproven prospect at 50% retention? Rebuild? Fold the team?

The cost of acquisition and the term of the contract make this worth doing, even if the AAV on Panarin's next deal doesn't do the team any favors in roster construction. It's a three-year window play for Kings GM Ken Holland. After that, it probably won't be his problem anyway. -- Wyshynski

The Vancouver Canucks couldn't wait any longer. The post-Quinn Hughes rebuild/retool is on.

GM Patrik Allvin lowered his first-round pick asking price for Kiefer Sherwood, and moved the forward to the San Jose Sharks for a pair of second-round selections (in 2026 and 2027) and defenseman Cole Clayton. Sherwood -- a pending unrestricted free agent -- had been available via for months before Allvin settled on a trade partner.

Let's take a look at how the trade benefits both sides.

Jamie Sabau/Getty Images

Canucks grade: B+

Vancouver knows where it's at, and that's currently 32nd in the NHL. Sherwood is a player who several teams coveted, and Allvin worked out quite a deal with San Jose GM Mike Grier.

The incoming draft selections give the Canucks two second-round picks in 2026 to go along with a pair of first-round choices. And they were missing a second-round pick in 2027, so this deal replaces that as well.

Allvin had to do his part in making it a smarter transaction for Grier, which is why he takes on Clayton's contract. Still, the 25-year-old is a right-shot defenseman who will immediately report to Vancouver's AHL team in Abbotsford but could potentially develop into a useful player for the Canucks as they go through a retooling phase.

This is another step for Vancouver in moving forward into its next era.

Sharks grade: B-

This was an interesting decision by the Sharks. Perhaps the grade here really should be "incomplete." Because San Jose has traded for an injured player (Sherwood hasn't been available since Jan. 10 due to an undisclosed ailment) who is set to become an unrestricted free agent in July.
